next_steps: Reconcile Morris's siglum through historical British Museum catalogs, accession and renumbering evidence, and reading-level comparison before adding any same-as or direct current-shelfmark edge.
Treat the current BL Harley MS 106 Latin theological codex and Washington's Latin prose loci as conflict evidence only until resolved.
openQuestions.question: What present physical object, if any, corresponds to the manuscript Morris cited as H106?

next_steps: Ask Princeton Special Collections for a carrier-specific written interpretation before redistributing the Garrett facsimile itself.
openQuestions.question: Does Princeton's current no-institutional-permission-required policy supersede the publication-permission instruction on the archived facsimile's embedded 1965 microfilm card?

next_steps: Ask U-M for the archived page-image package and a layer-specific rights statement; do not treat the dark Internet Archive record as a file delivery.
openQuestions.question: Can the historical 372-TIFF U-M page-image set be recovered with an image-layer reuse statement?

next_steps: Request item-specific quotes only as needed, naming the complete codex and separate target locus.
openQuestions.question: What whole-codex imaging scope, condition limits, color availability, price, and reuse permission would the BL offer for each identified witness?

next_steps: Seek a resource-level license; do not infer redistribution rights from the Open Access label alone.
openQuestions.question: What explicit reuse license, if any, applies to the METS Prik of Conscience Introduction and wider edition?

primaryTransmissionPaths.description: The repository artifact copies the U-M encoding; the header says that uncorrected OCR was created from the linked 372-TIFF image carrier, which reproduces Morris's edition.

primaryTransmissionPaths.description: Morris's main reading text follows Cotton Galba E IX except for explicitly separated supplies and paratext.

reviewNotes: The 29-entity, 17-relation graph is completely classified across five primary paths and three supporting groups.
The shared digital trunk is artifact → U-M OCR encoding → 372-TIFF carrier → Morris edition; unavailable TIFF payloads do not justify bypassing the image layer.
Morris's H106 is an unidentified historical witness; the current BL Harley MS 106 record is separate conflict evidence with no lineage edge.
The complete Garrett surrogate retains the whole codex ff. 1r–130v; only the table-of-contents relation is narrowed to ff. 1r–6v / PDF pp. 4–10.
Every one of the 33 cached files has exactly one delivery owner, exact URL, size, SHA-256, coverage, and explicit target-presence decision.

notes: Published per-image rates are not a whole-codex quote; large or difficult orders are assessed individually. Supplied images have separate permission terms.

sourceLinks.accessMethod: Use Princeton Special Collections' Ask Special Collections / consultation route for the physical codex.
Request fresh digitization only if the cached complete grayscale surrogate does not meet the research need.
notes: Packet rate: $2 per image for non-Princeton requesters, maximum 300 pages per project per six months, expected 6–8 weeks; verify current terms before ordering.

rights.basis: Current policy says Princeton permission and usage fees are not required, requests credit, and leaves third-party rights clearance to the user; the exact delivery terms for a future order remain request-stage evidence.
rights.component: Future newly supplied Princeton images
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms and intended place of reuse

sourceLinks.accessMethod: Ask the BL to reconcile Morris's historical H106 citation with the present cataloged codex before treating it as a target witness.
sourceLinks.entityLabel: Current British Library record for Harley MS 106

lineage.summary: The General Corpus XML copies U-M's uncorrected OCR, which was created from a 372-TIFF carrier of Morris's 1863 edition. Morris's main reading text follows Cotton Galba E IX, with two precisely scoped Harley 4196 supplies, eight additional cited collation sources, and a Garrett MS 138 table of contents. The graph preserves facsimiles, access routes, and rights layers without equating Morris's unresolved H106 with the current BL catalog record.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Around the middle of the fourteenth century (cautiously normalized 1325–1375)
catalogSummary.region: Probably northern England; this work-level inference is not a witness-dialect claim
catalogSummary.title: Prik of Conscience

catalogingSubject.label: Prik of Conscience
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English main or northern version represented by Morris's eclectic reading text. This is not Richard Rolle's authentic oeuvre, Morris's 1863 edition as a bibliographic object, one manuscript, the later Morey edition, or the whole contents of any composite witness.

contentParts.label: Prologue / entre
contentParts.summary: The opening treats creation, human nature, and the need for self-knowledge.

contentParts.label: Part I
contentParts.summary: The beginning, middle, end, and frailty of human life.

contentParts.label: Part II
contentParts.summary: The instability, vanity, and dangers of the world.

contentParts.label: Part III
contentParts.summary: Death and the reasons death should be feared.

contentParts.label: Part IV
contentParts.summary: Purgatory, sin, penance, and aid for the dead.

contentParts.label: Part V
contentParts.summary: The signs, process, and judgment of the Last Day.

contentParts.label: Part VI
contentParts.summary: The nature and pains of hell.

contentParts.label: Part VII
contentParts.summary: The joys and blessedness of heaven.

contentParts.label: Epilogue
contentParts.summary: A closing exhortation and prayer following the account of heaven.

dateStatements.display: around the middle of the fourteenth century
notes: Cautious work-level range; witness dates remain in lineage.

extent.description: One poem in nine internal divisions: an entre/prologue, seven parts, and an epilogue. The XML has 24 LG and 9,634 L elements, but L includes note-nested and encoding-level units while Morris's conventional numbering ends at 9,624; no false reconciled verse-line total is asserted.

formStatements.description: Didactic religious verse in rhymed four-stress couplets.

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: Modern English notes belong to the edition, not the medieval work.

scope.description: Embedded authorities and quotations throughout the poem

notes: The nine modeled parts are intellectual poem divisions; Morris's manuscript-derived table of contents is edition paratext represented in lineage, not a tenth part of the abstract work.
Latin quotations do not make the whole work multilingual for discovery; the primary discovery language remains Middle English.

next_steps: Build a position-by-position concordance separating poem text, supplied readings, and note-nested lines before recording a work-level verse-line total.
openQuestions.question: How should Morris's conventional lineation ending at 9,624 be reconciled with 9,634 XML L elements, including 30 L elements nested inside editorial notes?

notes: Do not substitute the location or dialect of a later witness.

assertion.notes: The once-common Rolle attribution is rejected; this does not assert authorship.

notes: Traditional attribution preserved only as catalog history.

assertion.notes: Historical manuscript attribution only; not accepted authorship.

scope.description: Immediate 1863 source edition rather than medieval authorship

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English didactic religious poem that moves from human wretchedness and worldly instability through death, purgatory, the Last Judgment, and hell to the joys of heaven. Its entre, seven parts, and epilogue combine pastoral instruction, moral theology, eschatology, and devotional exhortation in rhymed four-stress couplets.

The metadata describes the anonymous medieval work. Morris's eclectic edition, its table of contents copied from Garrett MS 138, the U-M OCR, scans, and physical witnesses are separate lineage layers.

notes: Edition-level historical spelling, not a separate work.

scope.description: Title of Morris's immediate source edition

notes: Latin alternate title; not evidence that the Middle English work's primary language is Latin.

work.author: Anonymous
work.dateDisplay: Around the middle of the fourteenth century (cautiously normalized 1325–1375)

publication.embeddedTitle: THE PRICKE OF CONSCIENCE (STIMULUS CONSCIENTIÆ) — A NORTHUMBRIAN POEM BY RICHARD ROLLE DE HAMPOLE.

work.regionDisplay: Probably northern England; this work-level inference is not a witness-dialect claim
work.summary: An anonymous Middle English didactic religious poem that moves from human wretchedness and worldly instability through death, purgatory, the Last Judgment, and hell to the joys of heaven. Its entre, seven parts, and epilogue combine pastoral instruction, moral theology, eschatology, and devotional exhortation in rhymed four-stress couplets.
